Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»Akani Simbine: ‘This is for everyone who wished it upon me’

It is a silver medal, but for what Akani Simbine has been through in his career it is worth its weight in gold.

It is a silver medal, but for whatAkani Simbinehas been through in his career it is worth its weight in gold.

Enjoy the Paris Olympic Games 2024 experience at your favourite ATKV Resort!

At the age of 30, the South African was the veteran in a side blessed with youth, exuberance and plenty of speed and he carried the hopes of a country over the line with an incredible run at the 2024 Paris Olympics.

Simbine is a three-time Olympian, having finished fifth in the 100m Rio final in 2016 then finding himself in the worst position in sport – fourth in an Olympic final – not once, but twice at Tokyo 2020 and Paris 2024.

However, a blistering final leg of 8.78 seconds saw him take Team South Africa from fifth to second, the silver medal being the fifth of the country’s campaign so far in Paris.

For the latest Paris Olympics 2024 news, bookmarkThe South African website’sdedicated section for free-to-read content

Afterwards, the South African speedster couldn’t hide his delight.

“This medal is for everyone who wished a medal upon me,” he said, basking in the afterglow of the most memorable performance of his career.

“I’ve finally got a major championship medal.

“My career isn’t defined by medals, it’s all been about consistency. I’ve always been an athlete who has stepped up to the plate and fought hard. A medal is just the cherry on the top for me. I am glad to be inspiring a new generation in South Africa, in Africa. My medal belongs to them, to the whole world.”

Canada crossed the line first in a time of 37.50, with Simbine stopping the clock at an African record 37.57.

Akani Simbine was joined in the celebrations which lasted long into the night by his team-mates who played their part in a memorable performance.

Bayanda Walaza is still 18 and at school, Shaun Maswanganyi is a two-time Olympian at the age of 23 and Bradley Nkoana is only 19.

That makes it two teenagers in a relay squad that sent out the loudest message to the world on an evening when the rain came again to Paris.

However, no downpour was ever going to dampen the spirits of a South African awesome foursome which has sprinted into history, the first African country to win a men’s 4x100m men’s relay Olympic medal.

Gary Lemkeis reporting live from Paris for the Team SA website for the duration of the Paris Olympics 2024
